statement.markdown: Write a function that validates the structure of nested HTML tags. The function should take a string of HTML tags as input, and return true if the tags are valid, and false otherwise. A valid structure is one where every opening tag has a corresponding closing tag, and the closing tags are in the reverse order of the opening tags. For example, the string '' is valid, but the string '' is not. Ignore any tags that are not nested.
statement.inputFormat: A string of HTML tags, where each tag is enclosed in angle brackets '<' and '>'.
statement.outputFormat: true if the HTML tags are valid, false otherwise.
statement.notes: This is a medium difficulty problem. You can use a stack data structure to solve it.

constraints.description: The number of HTML tags in the input string.

examples.explanation: The tags are valid because every opening tag has a corresponding closing tag, and the closing tags are in the reverse order of the opening tags.
The tags are not valid because the closing tag '' comes before the closing tag ''.

hints.text: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags.
When you encounter a closing tag, check if the top of the stack matches the closing tag.
If the stack is empty when you encounter a closing tag, or if the top of the stack does not match the closing tag, return false.

editorial.title: HTML Tag Validation
editorial.intuition: The intuition behind this problem is to use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, check if the top of the stack matches the closing tag.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to solve this problem is to use a brute force approach and manually check each tag. However, this approach is inefficient because it has a time complexity of O(n^2), where n is the number of tags.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, check if the top of the stack matches the closing tag.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, check if the top of the stack matches the closing tag. If the stack is empty when you encounter a closing tag, or if the top of the stack does not match the closing tag, return false.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is that the stack data structure keeps track of the opening tags, and the closing tags are in the reverse order of the opening tags.

commonMistakes: Not using a stack data structure
Not checking if the stack is empty when encountering a closing tag
